plotlines.name: The Tartar Thistle Frame Narrative
plotlines.description: The first-person narrator walks home through fields, struggles to pluck a crimson 'Tartar' thistle, then discovers a mutilated but unbroken thistle in a ploughed field — which triggers the memory of Hadji Murad. Establishes the central metaphor of indomitable life crushed by civilization.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes the controlling metaphor (vitality/resistance vs. destruction) and frames the entire novella as a remembered Caucasian episode.

plotlines.name: Authorial Narrator Commentary
plotlines.description: Tolstoy's narrator interjects — framing the tale as remembered/imagined, and moralizing on man as a destructive creature and on the fiction of hand-to-hand heroic combat that soldiers believe in despite knowing better.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Narrator steps out of the story to moralize about violence, heroism-myth, and the destructiveness of man.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Defection to the Russians
plotlines.description: Fleeing Shamil's murids, Hadji Murad hides in Sado's saklya, sends Bata and Khan Mahoma as envoys to Prince Vorontsov at Vozvizhensk, escapes the hostile aoul under fire, regroups with his four murids in the forest, and formally surrenders to Vorontsov at the Shalin glade on a tree-felling expedition.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Drives the entire A-story: the protagonist's high-stakes crossover from Shamil's camp to the Russian side, setting up the central political gamble of the novella.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Hidden Goal — Rescuing His Family from Shamil
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad entrusts Sado with a concealed note 'to be handed to my son' and dreams of using a Russian army to march on Shamil, seize him and his wives, and reclaim Avaria and Chechnya — planting the buried motive behind his defection.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Plants the true stakes beneath the political surrender — the family held hostage — that will drive the tragic second half.

plotlines.name: Shamil's Pursuit of Hadji Murad
plotlines.description: Shamil has issued a proclamation forbidding anyone in Chechnya from sheltering Hadji Murad on pain of death; the Makhmet aoul's elders assemble to detain him, forcing the midnight escape under gunfire.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes the antagonist-regime (Shamil) as a concrete, armed threat — not an abstract enemy — and justifies Hadji Murad's desperation.

plotlines.name: Sado's Hospitality and Risk
plotlines.description: Sado knowingly risks death to shelter Hadji Murad as his kunak, domestic rituals of hospitality unfold, then a neighbor's wife spots the arrival and Sado wakes Hadji Murad in time to flee — fulfilling the guest-bond.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Dramatizes Chechen kunak-code and contrasts personal honor with collective political fear.

plotlines.name: Avdeev — Soldier's Homesickness and Mortal Wound
plotlines.description: In ambush Avdeev confesses homesickness and regret for having volunteered in his brother's stead; the next morning during the wood-cutting skirmish — a skirmish that is essentially theatrical — he is shot in the stomach and carried back to the fort.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Gives the anonymous Russian rank-and-file a face and an inner life, so that the senseless wounding indicts the whole imperial enterprise.

plotlines.name: The Company Commander's Borrowing from the Cashbox
plotlines.description: In the ambush chat, Nikitin grumbles that the commander has again dipped into the company's communal cashbox for gambling losses; Panov and Avdeev defend him.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Micro-portrait of petty corruption and soldier self-management inside the imperial army — a thread of institutional rot at the bottom of the chain.

plotlines.name: The Vorontsov Household at Vozvizhensk
plotlines.description: At the fort the Vorontsovs host a late card game in Petersburg-grade luxury; the emissary's arrival interrupts the game, the prince breaks out champagne, Marya Vasilevna pries the secret out in French, and next morning Vorontsov rides out in cheerful mood to accept the surrender.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Establishes the Russian imperial upper-command as socially frivolous, status-driven, and pleased by Hadji Murad's defection as a career coup — contrast to Avdeev's ambush.

plotlines.name: Poltoratsky's Infatuation with Marya Vasilevna
plotlines.description: Poltoratsky loses hands at cards because Marya Vasilevna's nearness dazzles him, goes home ecstatic imagining her smile, then leads his company into the wood-felling skirmish in that same 'elated, carelessly gay' mood.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Links sexual/social elation to battlefield light-heartedness — the officer class treats war as theatre.

plotlines.name: Vovilo the Serf's Resentment
plotlines.description: Poltoratsky's serf-servant Vovilo, drunk at a name-day party, bitterly compares his indentured campaigning life to the ordnance sergeant's and fastens the door out of sleepy fear.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Shows the lowest rung of the Russian social order — an unfree domestic — mirroring the imperial hierarchy built above him.

plotlines.name: The Sleptsov Death and the Heroism Myth
plotlines.description: Officers on drums eat, drink porter, and discuss General Sleptsov's death — celebrating hand-to-hand valor that the narrator explicitly calls a fiction; moments later real, pointless bullets fly.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Explicit thematic critique of the romantic war-myth the officer class lives inside, set against the actual wounding of Avdeev.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Four Murids Assembled
plotlines.description: Eldar is introduced at Sado's; in the forest camp Khanefi, Gamzalo, Khan Mahoma join; all five ride with Hadji Murad into Russian custody, each sharply individuated at the Shalin glade.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Builds the retinue as a collective protagonist that will carry much of the novella's later action.

plotlines.name: The Envoy Mission to Vorontsov
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad dispatches Khan Mahoma with Chechen guide Bata; Panov's ambush intercepts them at the plane tree; Avdeev and Bondarenko escort them to the Colonel; they return with Vorontsov's promise to receive Hadji Murad as a guest.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Structural hinge connecting the Chechen and Russian storylines — a relay race of information that makes the surrender logistically possible.

notes: Chapters 1-5 establish the defection arc and fan out a wide social tableau (Chechen village, ambush, officers' drawing room, serf's cabin, forest camp, wood-felling skirmish). Most non-main threads (thistle frame, author digression, Sleptsov myth, Vovilo, company cashbox) are thematic/structural and cuttable individually, but their aggregate is Tolstoy's core critique — fusing them into composite 'imperial-tableau' scenes is the natural adaptation move. Poltoratsky and Marya Vasilevna are here introduced but their arcs extend well beyond chapter 5.
